  • Ipercromica: Ipermelanosi epidermica o dermica (es. efelidi, lentigo, melasma).
  • Ipocromica / Acromica: Ipopigmentazione quantitativa o totale per difetto di melanogenesi o perdita di melanociti (es. vitiligine, pitiriasi versicolor, albinismo).
  • Emorragica (Porpora, Ecchimosi, Petechia): Stravaso ematico intra-dermico da danno vascolare (vasculite) o piastrinopenia; non vitropressibile.
  • Pseudomacchia: Deposito di pigmenti endogeni (emosiderina, bilirubina, carotene) o esogeni (tatuaggi, metalli).

Nomenclatura dimensionale: Macchia (≤ 1 cm); Chiazza (> 1 cm o per confluenza di più macchie).

PAPULA / PLACCA:

Papula: Lesione solida, rilevata e ben circoscritta di diametro ≤ 1 cm. Può derivare da iperplasia epidermica (acantosi), infiltrato cellulare infiammatorio o neoplastico nel derma superficiale, o accumulo metabolico. Di norma regredisce senza esito cicatriziale.

Placca: Lesione solida, superficiale e stesa in superficie, con diametro > 1 cm, spesso formata dalla confluenza di più papule o dall'espansione centrifuga di un'infiltrazione dermica.

Esempi clinici: Lichen planus, mollusco contagioso, psoriasi (placche eritemato-squamose), eczema subacuto.

NODULO / NODOSITÀ:

Nodulo: Lesione solida, circoscritta, sferoidale o ovoide, di diametro > 1 cm, con sede prevalente nel derma medio-profondo (reticolare) o nell'ipoderma superficiale. Origina da processi neoplastici o infiammatori granulomatosi; può evolvere in necrosi e guarire con esito cicatriziale.

Nodosità: Processo infiltrativo solido, profondo, a sede dermo-ipodermica o ipodermica pura (pannicolite). Palpabile come massa dolente ed eritematosa; tipica dell'eritema nodoso.

POMFO:

Rilevatezza edematosa della cute, di forma e dimensioni variabili, ad insorgenza rapida e carattere fugace (risoluzione spontanea entro 24 ore senza reliti cicatriziali).

Patogenesi: Edema acuto del derma papillare causato da vasodilatazione e iperpermeabilità capillare, per lo più mediata dal rilascio di istamina da parte dei mastociti.

Sintomatologia e aspetto: Colore variabile dal rosso roseo al bianco-porcellanaceo (per compressione vascolare da edema). Associato a prurito intenso. Quadro patognomonico dell'orticaria e delle reazioni da puntura d'insetto.

VESCICOLA / BOLLA:

Raccolta circoscritta di liquido limpido (siero, plasma) o ematico, formatasi per scissione anatomica negli strati dell'epidermide o alla giunzione dermo-epidermica.

Vescicola: Diametro ≤ 5 mm. Deriva da edema intercellulare (spongiosi) o da citolisi virale (es. eczema acuto, herpes simplex, varicella zoster).

Bolla: Diametro > 5 mm. Può avere sede intraepidermica (es. pemfigo volgare) o sottoepidermica (es. pemfigoide bolloso). Il termine flittena individua clinicamente la bolla prodotta da insulti fisico-chimici (ustioni, attrito, agenti caustici).

PUSTOLA:

Raccolta circoscritta di essudato purulento (costituito da leucociti neutrofili, detriti cellulari e talvolta microrganismi) localizzata all'interno dell'epidermide o nell'ostio follicolare. Si presenta come una rilevatezza giallo-biancastra circondata da un alone eritematoso.

Pressione endogena variabile; evoluzione in crosta. Può essere settica (es. follicolite, impetigine) o asettica/sterile (es. psoriasi pustolosa, AGEP).

Lesioni Secondarie (Evolutive)

SQUAMA:

Accumulo visibile e desquamante di lamelle cornee. Riflette un'anomalia della cheratinizzazione (ipercheratosi) o un'accelerazione del turnover epidermico con ritenzione dei nuclei (paracheratosi).

Morfologia: Pitiriasica/furfuracea (sottile, farinosa), psoriasica (spessa, argentata, stratificata), lamellare o membranacea. Tipica di psoriasi, dermatite seborroica, ittiosi e micosi superficiali.

CROSTA:

Formazione solida derivante dall'essiccamento sulla superficie cutanea di essudato organico (siero, sangue o pus), misto a detriti cellulari epidermici. Surroga momentaneamente il rivestimento epiteliale leso.

Si distinguono croste siero-ematiche (marrone-nerastre), meliceriche (giallo-miele, tipiche dell'impetigine) o purulente (giallo-verdastre).

ESCORIAZIONE:

Soluzione di continuo di origine traumatica o meccanica (es. grattamento) che comporta la perdita parziale o totale della sola epidermide, al più limitata alle apici delle papille dermiche. Guarisce senza esito cicatriziale.

EROSIONE:

Perdita di sostanza epidermica superficiale, ad eziologia patologica (es. rottura di una vescicola o bolla intraepidermica). Conserva la membrana basale o il derma papillare integro; reepitelizza senza lasciare cicatrice.

ULCERAZIONE:

Perdita di sostanza profonda che interessa l'epidermide, la membrana basale e si estende al derma (reticolare) e talora all'ipoderma. Sostenuta da processi vascolari, infettivi o neoplastici. Risolve obbligatoriamente con esito cicatriziale.

RAGADE (Fissura):

Soluzione di continuo lineare, spesso dolente, che affonda perpendicolarmente nelle pliche o nei distretti a forte tensione meccanica (angoli della bocca, palmi, piante, zona anale). Interessa l'epidermide e il derma superficiale; può guarire con una cicatrice lineare reticolare.

CICATRICE:

Tessuto di neoformazione fibroso-collagenico che sostituisce la perdita di sostanza dermica o dermo-ipodermica. È caratterizzata dall'assenza totale di annessi cutanei (follicoli piliferi, ghiandole sebacee e sudoripare) e presenta un pattern vascolare e pigmentario alterato.

Tipologie: Può presentarsi in forma normotrofica (a livello della cute), atrofica (sotto il livello cutaneo), ipertrofica o cheloidea (estensione oltre i margini della lesione originale).

Note per il laboratorio: La diagnosi differenziale con l'atrofia cutanea semplice si basa proprio sulla presenza di annessi residui nell'atrofia, assenti nella cicatrice vera e propria.
it.hair_shaft_fractures_section:
ANOMALIE DEL FUSTO DEL CAPELLO: FRATTURE E DEFORMITÀ STRUTTURALI 💔

Le anomalie del fusto del capello sono alterazioni strutturali che compromettono la resistenza meccanica della fibra cheratinica, classificabili in base alla localizzazione del danno (cuticola, corteccia, midollo) e all'eziologia (acquisita/traumatica vs genetica/congenita). Rappresentano una causa primaria e frequente di alopecia da fragilità.

1. Tricorressi Nodosa (Frattura a Pennello)

Morfologia:
Perdita focale e sfiancamento della cuticola con conseguente esposizione e sfilacciamento delle fibrille corticali. Si formano noduli biancastri che, alla frattura trasversale, assumono il caratteristico aspetto "a pennello" (brush-like fracture), assimilabile a due scope incastrate l'una di fronte all'altra.
Eziologia:
Prevalenza acquisita da traumi fisico-chimici cumulativi (calore eccessivo, decolorazioni, permanenti, spazzolatura aggressiva). Più raramente congenita (es. argininosuccinaciduria, sindromi mitocondriali).

2. Tricoschìsi (Frattura Netta nella Tricotiodistrofia)

Morfologia:
Frattura trasversale netta e pulita della fibra senza sfilacciamento corticale. Il fusto appare appiattito e ruvido. Alla microscopia a luce polarizzata mostra il tipico pattern alternato a bande scure e chiare denominato "a coda di tigre" (tiger-tail pattern).
Eziologia:
Difetto congenito della cheratinizzazione caratterizzato da severa carenza di zolfo e drastica riduzione delle proteine matricali ricche di cistina (< 50% rispetto ai valori fisiologici).
Associazione:
Segno patognomonico della Tricotiodistrofia (TTD), una condizione autosomica recessiva spesso associata a ittiosi, fotosensibilità e ritardo dello sviluppo psico-motorio.

3. Tricorressi Invaginata ("Capelli a Bambù")

Morfologia:
Deformità da intussuscezione: il segmento distale del fusto, già cheratinizzato e rigido, si invagina nella porzione prossimale più morbida e incompletamente cheratinizzata, determinando una giunzione "a sfera e coppa" (ball-and-socket). In seguito a frattura, l'estremità prossimale assume il tipico aspetto a "testa da golf" (golf-tee hair).
Associazione:
Marcatore diagnostico patognomonico della Sindrome di Netherton (mutazione del gene SPINK5 con deficit dell'inibitore LEKTI), associata a ittiosi lineare circonflessa e diatesi atopica severa.

4. Capelli a Punta di Matita (Pencil-Point Hairs)

Morfologia:
Rottura che si verifica in corrispondenza di un repentino e progressivo assottigliamento del calibro del fusto. Il frammento residuo prossimale presenta un'estremità affusolata e assottigliata, definita "a punta di matita".
Associazione:
Spettro evolutivo dei capelli "a punto esclamativo" (tapered hairs) tipici dell'Alopecia Areata e dell'anagen distrofico da tossicità (es. chemioterapia), indice di una brusca e transitoria inibizione della mitosi della matrice del bulbo.

5. Tricoptilòsi (Doppia Punta)

Morfologia:
Fissurazione e sdoppiamento longitudinale della porzione distale o intermedia del fusto (comunemente nota come "doppia punta"). Rappresenta lo stadio finale del deperimento cuticolare (weathering) con sfaldamento e separazione delle macrofibrille della corteccia.
Eziologia:
Esclusivamente di origine acquisita, provocata dal degrado chimico-fisico progressivo e da sollecitazioni meccaniche ripetute sull'estremità distale del capello.

6. Tricoclasìa (Frattura a Legno Verde)

Morfologia:
Frattura trasversale incompleta della corteccia e del midollo, con preservazione dell'involucro cuticolare, il quale rimane intatto a fare da "ponte" sulla fessurazione. Nota anche come frattura "a legno verde" (greenstick fracture).
Etiologia:
Risultato di traumi fisici di moderata entità o piegamenti meccanici che provocano il cedimento della struttura interna senza rompere la guaina cuticolare esterna.
it.hair_shaft_deformities_section:
ALTERAZIONI DELLA REGOLARITÀ E STRUTTURA DEL FUSTO 📏

Queste anomalie interessano la morfologia longitudinale o trasversale del capello, derivando principalmente da difetti della cheratinizzazione o dell'attività mitotica nel bulbo follicolare. La distinzione clinica fondamentale risiede nella regolarità del difetto (spesso genetica) rispetto all'irregolarità (spesso acquisita o traumatica).

1. Moniletrix (Capelli a Collana)

Morfologia:
Fusto con variazione periodica del diametro: nodi ellittici (spessi, simili a capelli normali) alternati a internodi affusolati (sottili, privi di midollo). La cuticola è spesso integra sui nodi ma difettosa sugli internodi, dove avviene la frattura. Alla tricoscopia, i nodi appaiono regolari e uniformemente distanziati.
Eziologia:
Difetto genetico della cheratinizzazione. Forma Autosomica Dominante (mutazioni geni KRT81, KRT83, KRT86) o Autosomica Recessiva (mutazioni gene DSG4, codificante la Desmogleina 4). Spesso associato a cheratosi follicolare.

2. Pseudomoniletrix

Differenze Chiave dal Moniletrix:
Considerato un artefatto più che una patologia genetica. Si distingue per:
  • Rigonfiamenti di dimensioni e distanza irregolari (non periodici).
  • Cellule cuticolari generalmente conservate e intatte.
  • Origine acquisita (traumi meccanici da spazzolatura o compressione), non ereditaria.
  • Le fratture, se presenti, si verificano prevalentemente a livello dei nodi (punti di stress).

3. Pili Torti (Capelli Ritorti)

Morfologia:
Fusto appiattito con torsioni assiali di 90°–180° (fino a 360°) a intervalli irregolari (0.5–1 mm). La sezione diventa ellittica nei punti ritorti. Estremamente fragili e corti (lunghezza spesso < 5 cm). Alla luce incidente mostrano un riflesso scintillante ("sparkling").
Eziologia:
Può essere isolata (mutazioni gene BCS1L nella Sindrome di Björnstad) o sindromica. Forma classica associata alla Sindrome di Menkes (mutazione gene ATP7A, carenza di rame, trasmissione X-linked recessiva). Può essere acquisita da traumi o infiammazione.
Diagnosi:
Alla luce polarizzata, le torsioni creano bande chiare e scure alternate. Segno cardine per lo screening di difetti del metabolismo del rame.

4. Capelli Affusolati (Tapered Hairs)

Morfologia e Correlazione Clinica:
Presenza di restringimenti progressivi lungo il fusto fino a terminare con una punta sottile mantenendo la cuticola intatta. Rappresentano la manifestazione primaria dell'assottigliamento della matrice: quando il punto di massimo restringimento va incontro a rottura, si generano i capelli "a punta di matita" (o "a punto esclamativo"), descritti nella sezione precedente come entità di frattura.
Eziologia:
Indica un rallentamento improvviso o arresto temporaneo dell'attività mitotica della matrice follicolare. Marker di attività nell'Alopecia Areata, ma presente anche in tricotillomania, gravi malattie sistemiche o terapie citotossiche.

5. Scanalature Longitudinali (Pili trianguli et canaliculi)

Morfologia:
Il termine ufficiale descrive due caratteristiche: Trianguli (sezione trasversale triangolare o a rene) e Canaliculi (presenza di una scanalatura/canale longitudinale). La cuticola rimane intatta sopra il solco. In sezione trasversale, il capello appare triangolare.
Significato Clinico:
È il marcatore patognomonico della Sindrome dei Capelli Impettinabili (UHS, Uncombable Hair Syndrome), nota anche come "sindrome dei capelli di lana di vetro". L'aspetto è tipico: capelli secchi, lucenti (colore biondo-paglia o argento), crespi e impossibili da districare. Spesso associata a Tricotiodistrofia o displasie ectodermiche.

6. Pili Annulati (Capelli Anellati)

Morfologia:
Fusto con bande alternate chiare e scure ("aspetto zebrato").
Luce riflessa: le bande appaiono chiare (luccicanti).
Luce trasmessa: le stesse bande appaiono scure.
Causato da microcavità piene d'aria nella corteccia dovute a disallineamento cellulare.
Eziologia e Diagnosi Differenziale:
Generalmente ereditaria (autosomica dominante, locus 12q), ma può essere sporadica. A differenza della Tricotiodistrofia (trattata nella sezione precedente), i capelli non sono fragili e l'alterazione è prevalentemente estetica.

7. Pili Bifurcati

Morfologia:
Il fusto si divide in due rami distinti, ciascuno con propria cuticola, per poi eventualmente ricongiungersi più distalmente.
Eziologia:
Ipotesi di formazione di una matrice secondaria temporanea o duplicazione parziale della papilla dermica durante l'anagen. Differisce dalla tricoptilòsi (descritta nella sezione "Fratture"), che è una fessurazione acquisita di un singolo fusto.

8. Pili Multigemini

Morfologia:
Anomalia rara con 2–10 fusti piliferi che emergono da un unico ostio follicolare. Ogni fusto ha la propria cuticola e guaina epiteliale interna, ma condividono una guaina epiteliale esterna comune.
Diagnosi Differenziale:
Distinto dalla Tricostasi Spinulosa (aggregati di capelli vellus telogen intrappolati in un follicolo dilatato da un tappo di cheratina) e dal Follicolo Composto (più follicoli indipendenti che convergono). Nei Pili Multigemini, tutti i fusti sono solitamente in fase anagen strutturata.
it.anomalie_residue_sezione:
ANOMALIE RESIDUE E ALTERAZIONI DELLA CURVATURA DEL FUSTO ➰

Questa sezione raggruppa difetti acquisiti da trauma meccanico (annodamento) e anomalie congenite che alterano la geometria trasversale o la curvatura del fusto, influenzandone la pettinabilità e l'aspetto estetico senza necessariamente indurre fragilità da frattura.

1. Triconodòsi (Capello Annodato / Trichonodosis)

Definizione:
Anomalia esclusivamente acquisita caratterizzata dalla formazione di veri e propri nodi lungo il fusto (single-strand knots). A livello della spira del nodo, la tensione meccanica causa lo sfaldamento della cuticola con esposizione e sfilacciamento della corteccia.
Patogenesi e Biomeccanica:
Deriva da traumi meccanici ripetuti (spazzolatura aggressiva, frizione). La sezione trasversale fortemente appiattita o ellittica riduce il momento d'inerzia alla torsione, favorendo l'arricciamento autogeno e l'annodamento ("effetto molla").
Fattori di Rischio:
Prevalente in chi ha capelli ricci, crespi o molto fini (etnia africana o capelli caucasici ricci). Raramente spontanea in capelli estremamente lunghi e trascurati.

2. Sindrome dei Capelli Impettinabili (Uncombable Hair Syndrome - UHS)

Quadro clinico pediatrico correlato al segno microscopico dei Pili trianguli et canaliculi (v. sezione precedente), con graduale risoluzione spontanea durante la pubertà.

Eziologia Molecolare:
Patologia genetica (prevalenza autosomica recessiva) legata a mutazioni nei geni del complesso di cheratinizzazione follicolare: PADI3, TGM3, TCHH e il più recente SULT2B1.
Cascata Biochimica e Fisiopatologia:
La peptidilarginina deiminasi 3 (PADI3) deimina la tricoialina (TCHH) permettendone il legame con i filamenti di cheratina; la transglutaminasi 3 (TGM3) catalizza poi i legami crociati isopeptidici stabili. Un difetto in questo asse provoca la cheratinizzazione prematura della guaina epiteliale interna, che deforma e rigidifica il fusto ancora malleabile.
Correlato Clinico:
Capelli secchi, disordinati e proiettati verso l'esterno, impossibili da appiattire ma privi di un significativo incremento della fragilità alla trazione.

3. Capelli Lanosi (Woolly Hair)

Morfologia e Diagnosi Differenziale:
Fusti con sezione ovoidale/appiattita, caratterizzati da torsioni assiali strette e curvature irregolari. Rispetto ai Pili Torti (v. sezione precedente), le torsioni si presentano meno strutturate e la resistenza meccanica del capello è ampiamente conservata.
Classificazione e Genetica:
Ereditaria (Generalizzata): Coinvolge l'intero cuoio capelluto. Autosomica Dominante o Recessiva; le forme recessive sono spesso marcatore di sindromi cardio-cutanee gravemente invalidanti (es. Sindrome di Naxos o di Carvajal per mutazioni nei geni desmosomiali JUP o DSP).
Localizzata (Woolly Hair Nevus): Chiazza circoscritta su cuoio capelluto sano, non ereditaria, talvolta in mosaicismo associato a nevi epidermici.
Acquisita (o Kinky Hair indotto): Rara, secondaria a farmaci (retinoidi sistemici come acitretina/isotretinoina, inibitori di EGFR) o nelle fasi iniziali di alopecia androgenetica.
Fisiopatologia:
Marcata riduzione della fase anagen (lunghezza massima del capello spesso < 5 cm). L'impossibilità di pettinaggio è legata alla densità spaziale delle micro-curvature e non a frattura del fusto.
it.seborrea_intro: La seborrea è definita come un'aumentata produzione di sebo (seborrhea oleosa) o un'alterazione qualitativa della sua composizione lipidica (seborrhea sicca). Non è una malattia di per sé, ma una disfunzione fisiologica che altera il film idrolipidico, creando un substrato favorevole per condizioni patologiche come l'acne, la forfora e la dermatite seborroica.
it.seborrea_fisiologica_d: Normale attività delle ghiandole olocrine necessaria per formare il mantello idro-lipidico (protezione barriera) e lubrificare il fusto del capello. Varia in base all'età, al sesso e alla genetica.
it.seborrea_patologica_d: Iperproduzione quantitativa di sebo che rende cute e capelli untuosi. Può portare a ritenzione sebacea (sebo ossidato che ristagna nei follicoli), formazione di comedoni e alterazione del microbiota cutaneo.
it.seborrea_secca_d: Alterazione qualitativa del sebo: carenza di lipidi essenziali (es. acido linoleico) e aumento di squalene perossidato. La superficie appare secca e squamosa nonostante l'attività ghiandolare, spesso associata a barriera cutanea compromessa.
it.cause_ormonali_d: Il principale regolatore è il 5-alfa-diidrostosterone (DHT), derivato dal testosterone tramite l'enzima 5-alfa-reduttasi di tipo I presente nel sebocita. Il DHT stimola la proliferazione dei sebociti e la sintesi lipidica (tramite attivazione di SREBP). Anche IGF-1 e insulina possono potenziare questo effetto.
it.cause_composizione_d: Nel sebo "acneico" o patologico si osserva spesso una riduzione di acido linoleico (essenziale per la coesione follicolare) e un aumento di squalene e cere. Lo squalene è altamente suscettibile all'ossidazione, generando radicali liberi pro-infiammatori.
it.fattori_microbici_d: Il lievito lipofilo Malassezia (specie globosa e restricta) prolifera nel sebo in eccesso. I suoi enzimi lipasi idrolizzano i trigliceridi sebacei rilasciando acidi grassi liberi (es. acido oleico) che, penetrando nello strato corneo, inducono irritazione, infiammazione e iperproliferazione dei cheratinociti (forfora/dermatite).
it.seborrea_alopecia: Alopecia Seborroica / Androgenetica: La seborrea è un cofattore di rischio. L'infiammazione perifollicolare indotta dal sebo ossidato e dalla Malassezia può accelerare il miniaturizzazione del follicolo nei soggetti geneticamente predisposti.
it.seborrea_follicolite: Follicolite e Acne: L'ostruzione follicolare da ipercheratosi e sebo denso favorisce la proliferazione di Cutibacterium acnes e l'infiammazione pustolosa.
it.seborrea_pitiriasi: Pitiriasi Steatoide: Forma intermedia tra forfora secca e dermatite seborroica. Caratterizzata da squame untuose, giallastre e aderenti, impregnate di sebo ossidato.
it.seborrea_dermatite: Dermatite Seborroica: Condizione infiammatoria cronica (eritema + desquamazione) in zone sebacee (cuoio capelluto, sopracciglia, ali del naso). Distinta dalla semplice seborrea per la presenza di infiammazione attiva e prurito.
it.seborrea_terapia_intro: Il trattamento mira a ridurre la carica microbica, normalizzare il turnover cheratinocitario e regolare la secrezione sebacea senza compromettere la barriera cutanea (evitando l'effetto rebound da aggressione).
it.terapia_antimicrobica_d:
Ketoconazolo / Ciclopirox Olamina: Riducono drasticamente la popolazione di Malassezia.
Zinco Piritione / Piroctone Olamina: Azione antifungina e antibatterica, con lieve effetto citostatico sulla proliferazione dei cheratinociti.
it.terapia_cheratolitica_d:
Acido Salicilico: Favorisce la desquamazione delle squame e penetra nel follicolo.
Zolfo / Argille: Azione sebassorbente e lievemente antisettica.
it.terapia_sebo_regolatrice_d:
Antiandrogeni topici/systemici: (es. Serenoa Repens, Spironolattone) agiscono sulla via del DHT.
Corticosteroidi topici: Usati brevemente per spegnere l'infiammazione acuta nella dermatite seborroica.
Niacinamide / Tè Verde: Antiossidanti che riducono la perossidazione lipidica e l'infiammazione.
it.seborrea_fisioterapia: L'associazione con fisioterapie come l'Alta Frequenza (azione disinfettante ozonizzante e sebostatica) o la Laserterapia (biostimolazione e antinfiammatoria) può potenziare l'efficacia dei trattamenti topici.
it.forfora_intro: La forfora è una condizione cronica non infiammatoria (o lievemente infiammatoria) caratterizzata da desquamazione visibile del cuoio capelluto. È causata da un accelerato turnover epidermico: il tempo di ricambio dei cheratinociti si riduce da circa 28 giorni a 2–7 giorni. Le cellule non raggiungono la completa maturazione e si aggregano in squame visibili prima di distaccarsi.
it.forfora_fattori_titolo: Fisiopatologia: Il Ruolo del Microbiota e della Barriera
it.forfora_malassezia_d: La forfora richiede la presenza del lievito lipofilo Malassezia (specie M. globosa e M. restricta). Questo micete possiede enzimi lipasi che idrolizzano i trigliceridi del sebo, rilasciando acidi grassi liberi (es. acido oleico). Nei soggetti suscettibili, l'acido oleico penetra nello strato corneo, inducendo irritazione, infiammazione sub-clinica e iperproliferazione dei cheratinociti (paracheratosi).
it.forfora_barriera_d: Lo strato corneo forforaico presenta una funzione barriera compromessa e una maggiore permeabilità (TEWL aumentato). Questo favorisce la penetrazione di agenti irritanti e la perdita di acqua, creando un circolo vizioso di secchezza e desquamazione.
it.forfora_suscettibilità_d: Non tutti i portatori di Malassezia sviluppano forfora. La condizione dipende da una triade: eccesso di sebo (substrato), presenza del micete e suscettibilità individuale (genetica, stato immunitario, integrità della barriera).
it.forfora_secca_d: Squame fini, bianche o grigiastre, polverulente e poco aderenti. La cute sottostante è generalmente normale o lievemente xerotica. Prurito lieve o assente. Tipica della stagione invernale (aria secca).
it.forfora_grassa_d: Squame grosse, untuose e giallastre, spesso aggregate in placche aderenti. La cute sottostante è eritematosa, oleosa e infiammata. È considerata una forma lieve di dermatite seborroica e richiede un trattamento più aggressivo.
it.forfora_terapia_intro: L'obiettivo terapeutico è controllare la population di Malassezia, normalizzare il turnover cheratinocitario e ridurre l'infiammatorie, ripristinando al contempo la barriera cutanea.
it.forfora_antimicotici_d:
Ketoconazolo (2%): Azolo ad ampio spettro, inibisce la sintesi dell'ergosterolo nella membrana fungina.
Piritione di Zinco (ZPT): Agente multifunzionale con attività antifungina, antibatterica e lieve effetto citostatico (riduce la proliferazione dei cheratinociti).
Ciclopirox Olamina / Solfuro di Selenio: Alternative efficaci per ceppi resistenti o trattamenti di mantenimento.
it.forfora_cheratolitici_d:
Acido Salicilico: Favorisce la desquamazione chimica ("scollamento" delle squame) e penetra nel follicolo. Non ha attività antifungina diretta, ma potenzia l'accesso degli antimicotici.
Zolfo: Azione cheratolitica e sebostatica.
it.forfora_naturali_d: Tea Tree Oil (Melaleuca alternifolia): Dimostrata attività antifungina contro le specie di Malassezia in studi clinici, utile come coadiuvante.
it.forfora_ph_paragrafo: È fondamentale utilizzare shampoo con un pH acido (4.0–5.5) per rispettare il mantello idrolipidico fisiologico e creare un ambiente sfavorevole alla proliferazione fungina (che preferisce pH neutri/alcalini).
it.forfora_tempo_posa_paragrafo: Il tempo di contatto è cruciale: lo shampoo terapeutico deve agire per 3–5 minuti prima del risciacquo. Studi dimostrano che un contatto inferiore a 2 minuti riduce drasticamente l'efficacia antimicotica e citostatica dei principi attivi.
it.simple_title_main: CAPELLI: COME SONO FATTI E IL LORO CICLO VITALE
it.simple_anatomy_p1: I peli e i capelli sono filamenti fatti di cheratina. Nascono da un "sacco" nella pelle chiamato FOLLICOLO PILIFERO.
it.simple_anatomy_p2: Il follicolo si trova sotto la pelle del cuoio capelluto (derma). La sua parte finale, concava, si chiama BULBO.
it.simple_anatomy_p3: La lunghezza, lo spessore e la posizione dei capelli cambiano in base a dove si trovano, al sesso, all'età e alla razza.
it.simple_part_fusto: La parte che esce dalla pelle si chiama FUSTO (o STELO).
it.simple_part_radice: La parte sotto la pelle (dentro il follicolo) si chiama RADICE.
it.simple_part_papilla: In fondo alla radice c'è la PAPILLA DERMICA. Questa papilla porta il nutrimento al capello.
it.simple_part_matrice: Tra il bulbo e la radice c'è la MATRICE DEL CAPELLO. La matrice crea tutti i componenti del capello.
it.simple_follicle_text1: Il follicolo è un "sacco" della pelle, inclinato, che scende nel derma per 1-3 mm. Contiene il pelo e le sue guaine.
it.simple_follicle_text2: Ogni persona ha circa 5 milioni di follicoli. Circa 100.000 sono sul cuoio capelluto. Questo numero è stabilito prima della nascita e non aumenta più.
it.simple_gland_text1: Questa ghiandola produce il sebo, una sostanza grassa (trigliceridi, colesterolo, ecc.).
it.simple_gland_text2: Il sebo serve a lubrificare, ammorbidire e proteggere il capello man mano che cresce. Il sebo si diffonde sulla pelle e sul capello. La sua attività è controllata da fattori ormonali e alimentari.
it.simple_muscle_text1: È fatto di fibre muscolari lisce. Si attacca al follicolo.
it.simple_muscle_text2: Quando si contrae (per freddo o emozioni come paura), il pelo si mette dritto. Questo causa la pelle d'oca. Aiuta anche a far uscire il sebo.
it.simple_structure_p1: Il capello è una fibra complessa. Contiene acqua (10-30%) e grassi (sebo, cere, ecc.) per proteggersi.
it.simple_structure_p2: Il fusto del capello ha tre strati:
it.simple_layer_cuticola: CUTICOLA: È la parte più esterna. È fatta di cellule piatte e sovrapposte, come le tegole di un tetto.
it.simple_layer_corteccia: CORTECCIA: Si trova sotto la cuticola. È la parte più grande del capello. Dà al capello la sua resistenza e contiene la melanina (colore).
it.simple_layer_midollo: MIDOLLO: È la parte centrale, quasi assente nel fusto.
it.simple_structure_p3: Le molecole principali sono: cheratina (struttura), melanina (colore) e minerali.
it.simple_keratin_p1: Il capello è fatto principalmente di cheratina. Ne esistono due tipi:
it.simple_keratin_filamentosa: Cheratina Filamentosa: Forma dei fasci (fibrille) nella corteccia.
it.simple_keratin_amorfa: Cheratina Amorfa: Riempie gli spazi tra i fasci, dando stabilità.
it.simple_stability_p1: Le catene di cheratina sono unite da dei "ponti" che le tengono insieme. Ci sono 3 tipi di ponti:
it.simple_bridge_hydrogen_text: Sono legami molto deboli. Si rompono facilmente con l'acqua o il calore. Danno al capello flessibilità.
it.simple_bridge_saline_text: Sono legami mediamente forti. Si rompono con forte calore o con sostanze acide/basiche. Danno al capello resistenza.
it.simple_bridge_disulfide_text1: Sono legami molto forti (legami covalenti), fatti da atomi di zolfo.
it.simple_bridge_disulfide_text2: Questi ponti danno struttura (ad esempio i capelli ricci naturali). Possono essere rotti solo con molta energia (es. trattamenti chimici).
it.simple_bridge_disulfide_text3: I tre tipi di legame contribuiscono tutti alla resistenza del capello.
it.simple_minerals_p1: I minerali sono essenziali. Se mancano, i capelli diventano fragili, secchi e opachi.
it.simple_mineral_ferro: FERRO: Se manca, causa fragilità e caduta eccessiva.
it.simple_mineral_rame: RAME: Fondamentale per il colore (melanina) e per i ponti disolfuro.
it.simple_mineral_zinco: ZINCO: Indispensabile per la crescita delle cellule.
it.simple_lipids_p1: I lipidi provengono per lo più dal sebo.
it.simple_lipids_p2: I fosfolipidi sono importanti perché aiutano a mantenere la corretta idratazione del capello.
it.simple_melanin_p1: La melanina è il pigmento naturale. Si trova nella corteccia ed è prodotta dai melanociti.
it.simple_melanin_feomelanin: FEOMELANINA: Colore giallo/rosso (capelli biondi e rossi).
it.simple_melanin_tricochromes: TRICOCROMI: Pigmenti speciali per il colore rosso intenso.
it.simple_melanin_p3: La melanina nasce dall'aminoacido tirosina. La diversa percentuale dei pigmenti determina il colore finale del capello.
it.simple_cycle_p1: Il capello ha cicli di crescita e di riposo. Quando un capello vecchio cade, il follicolo ne forma uno nuovo.
it.simple_anagen_p1: È la fase più lunga. La matrice del capello è molto attiva e lo fa crescere.
it.simple_anagen_growth: Il capello cresce di circa 1-1,5 cm al mese.
it.simple_catagen_p1: È molto breve, dura 2-4 settimane. La matrice smette di lavorare.
it.simple_telogen_p1: È il periodo di riposo, dura circa 3 mesi.
it.simple_telogen_p2: Il capello cade solo quando ne sta crescendo uno nuovo che lo spinge fuori.
it.simple_figure_cycle_caption: Le tre fasi del ciclo di vita del capello.
it.simple_cycle_p3: La caduta dei capelli nell'uomo è a "mosaico": ogni follicolo segue il suo ciclo da solo. Questo assicura che non ci siano periodi senza capelli.
it.simple_cycle_note: Nota: ogni follicolo può fare fino a 30 cicli di vita.

en.title_main: HAIR: ANATOMY, STRUCTURE, AND LIFE CYCLE
en.anatomy_origin:
Hair consists of filamentous, keratinized cutaneous appendages originating from the HAIR FOLLICLES: oblique invaginations of the epidermis that penetrate the dermis, terminating at the base in an expanded, concave structure known as the HAIR BULB. Morphology, topographic distribution, and shaft thickness are determined by genetic, anatomical, hormonal, and ethnic factors.

Anatomically, the pilary unit is divided into three distinct portions: the SHAFT, the visible segment emerging from the skin surface; the ROOT, the intra-follicular portion; and the BULB, the deep basal portion. Within the concavity of the bulb resides the DERMAL PAPILLA, a conical connective tissue structure, richly vascularized and innervated, essential for nourishment and regulation of the follicular cycle.

The lower portion of the bulb surrounding the papilla constitutes the HAIR MATRIX (germinative layer). This population of undifferentiated cells, exhibiting high mitotic activity, is responsible for protein synthesis and the progressive keratinization that generates all hair components.

The combination of the follicle, the hair, and adjacent glandular and muscular structures constitutes the morpho-functional unit known as the PILO-SEBACEOUS APPARATUS, whose proper function ensures the integrity of the cutaneous barrier and tissue homeostasis.
en.figure1_caption: Anatomical section of the PILOSEBACEOUS APPARATUS.
en.pilosebaceous_components:
The PILO-SEBACEOUS APPARATUS comprises three fundamental structures:

  • The HAIR FOLLICLE, housing the hair root and its associated sheaths.
  • The ARRECTOR PILI (ERECTOR PILI) MUSCLE.
  • The SEBACEOUS GLAND (which, in certain locations, is associated with an apocrine sweat gland).

HAIR FOLLICLE: A specialized epidermal structure, obliquely oriented relative to the skin surface, penetrating the dermis to a depth of 1–3 mm. The total number of follicles in humans is genetically determined, amounting to approximately 5 million, with about 100,000 localized on the scalp. Follicular morphogenesis is an early process: it begins between the 9th and 12th weeks of gestation and is completed by the 7th month of fetal life; thereafter, no new follicles form de novo.

SEBACEOUS GLAND: A holocrine exocrine gland that secretes sebum into the follicular infundibulum via the sebaceous duct. Sebum, a lipid mixture rich in triglycerides, squalene, waxes, and cholesterol, lubricates, hydrates, and waterproofs the hair cuticle, protecting the skin. Located in the upper portion (infundibulum) of the follicle, its activity does not interfere with the mitotic processes of the hair bulb matrix. Sebum secretion is finely regulated by endocrine factors (primarily androgens), as well as neural and metabolic stimuli.

ARRECTOR PILI (ERECTOR PILI) MUSCLE: Composed of a bundle of smooth muscle fibers, it anchors to the papillary dermis and inserts into the connective tissue sheath of the follicle, just above the sebaceous gland. Its involuntary contraction, mediated by the sympathetic nervous system in response to cold or emotional stimuli (fear, stress), causes the verticalization of the hair shaft (piloerection or "goosebumps"). This mechanical pressure on the underlying sebaceous gland actively facilitates the expulsion of sebum into the follicular lumen.
en.secHeader_structure: PILOSEBACEOUS APPARATUS IN DETAIL - THE HAIR: STRUCTURE
en.hair_structure_detail:
The hair shaft is a complex biological structure with a high degree of organization, composed of specific macromolecular and chemical components. Its hydration rate varies from 10% to 30% by weight, closely related to environmental thermohygrometric conditions. The cuticular surface is covered by a lipid film of sebum (composed of triglycerides, waxes, cholesterol, phospholipids, and squalene), which performs an essential barrier function against external chemical-physical insults.

The hair shaft is structured into three concentric layers:

  • Cuticle: the outermost layer, formed by flattened, heavily keratinized cells (up to 6–8 overlapping layers), arranged longitudinally like roof tiles to protect the underlying cortex.
  • Cortex: located beneath the cuticle, it constitutes the predominant component of the shaft. It is formed by fusiform cells rich in aligned keratin filaments, determining the mechanical properties of resistance and elasticity of the hair. It also houses melanin granules, responsible for pigmentation.
  • Medulla: the central column of the shaft, often discontinuous or absent in very fine hairs. It consists of loosely connected keratinized cells arranged in rows and interspersed with air-filled vacuoles (micro-air spaces) and pigment traces.
en.figure2_caption: Detailed representation of the complete Pilosebaceous Apparatus.
en.hair_biochemistry:
From a biochemical perspective, the fundamental constituents of hair (besides water) include keratin (predominantly in hydrophobic α-conformation), structural and surface lipids, melanin pigments (eumelanins and pheomelanins), and a fraction of trace elements and minerals essential in atomic state (such as iron, zinc, magnesium, and calcium).

KERATIN

Hair keratins are classified into two types based on structural characteristics: fibrillar keratin and amorphous protein matrix. Fibrillar keratin forms a complex ultrastructure organized into bundles or fibrils in the cortex, while the amorphous protein matrix fills the voids between fibrils, stabilizing their architecture. Polypeptide chains coil upon themselves, assuming a spiral shape similar to DNA.

MECHANISMS OF ULTRASTRUCTURAL STABILITY

Keratin macromolecules, composed of long polypeptide chains, are stabilized and laterally held together by interchain chemical bonds (bridges) established between amino acid residues of adjacent filaments.

Three main types of structural interactions are distinguished:

HYDROGEN BONDS (BRIDGES)

Weak electrostatic interactions, easily cleaved by water and heat (phenomenon at the basis of temporary thermal or wet set styling). They are established mainly between the oxygen atom of the carbonyl group (-C=O) and the hydrogen atom of the amide group (-NH-) of the peptide backbone. Intrachain, they stabilize the α-helix conformation; interchain, they confer flexibility and reversible elasticity to the hair fiber.

SALT BRIDGES (IONIC INTERACTIONS)

Medium-energy bonds, sensitive to extreme temperature variations and, especially, to sudden pH changes (strongly acidic or basic solutions). These are electrostatic interactions between ionized functional groups of side chains of acidic amino acids (e.g., glutamic acid, aspartic acid) and basic amino acids (e.g., lysine, arginine) belonging to adjacent α-helices. Numerically superior to disulfide bridges, they are fundamental for structural stability and tensile resistance.

DISULFIDE BRIDGES

Strong covalent bonds formed by the oxidation of thiol groups (-SH) of two cysteine residues on adjacent polypeptide chains, resulting in the formation of a cystine molecule (-S-S-). These bridges stably crosslink both internal structures within microfibrils (α-helices) and the surrounding amorphous protein matrix (sulfur-rich), ensuring cohesion and biomechanical integrity of the entire hair ultrastructure.

The disulfide bridge is the primary factor stabilizing the tertiary and quaternary structure of keratin, determining rigidity, chemical-physical resistance, and morphology (curl) of hair and nails. In the cortex of natural curly hair, the asymmetric arrangement and frequency of these bridges induce protein chains to bend and fold permanently. Cleavage requires energetically demanding redox processes, exploited in chemical treatments (perms, chemical straightening, bleaching, oxidative coloring).

Although salt and hydrogen bonds have lower bond energy than disulfide bridges, their extremely high numerical density means that each of the three classes contributes approximately 1/3 to the overall mechanical resistance and shape stability of the hair.

The spatial configuration, density, and geometric regularity with which these bonds distribute among keratin filaments (particularly the architecture of disulfide bridges) determine the macro-molecular organization of the cortex and, consequently, the morphological phenotype (straight, wavy, curly) of the hair.

TRACE ELEMENTS AND MINERALS

Minerals constitute essential biochemical and catalytic components for hair homeostasis. Mineral deficiency states directly alter keratogenesis, inducing structural fragility, dryness, loss of luster, and follicular cycle anomalies (telogen effluvium).

The main trace elements involved are:

  • IRON (4-12 μg/g): Essential enzymatic cofactor; deficiency (sideropenia) compromises bulbar matrix cell proliferation, causing fragility and effluvium. Typically elevated concentrations in red hair (pheomelanin).
  • MAGNESIUM (30-45 μg/g): Acts as an activator in numerous enzymatic complexes and ATP synthesis, fundamental for hair protein biosynthesis. Concentrated mainly in dark hair.
  • COPPER (16-50 μg/g): Constitutes the catalytic core of the tyrosinase enzyme, indispensable for melanogenesis. Indirectly participates in structural stability by catalyzing disulfide bridge formation in keratin.
  • ZINC (150-180 μg/g): Trace element fundamental for polymerase activity and germinal cell division in the bulbar matrix. Chronic deficiency inhibits cellular turnover, causing marked fragility and shaft dystrophy.

The endogenous mineral component also regulates the intrinsic hydration of the hair fiber through hygroscopic equilibrium maintenance; loss of bound water alters the spatial arrangement of macrofibrils, compromising structural cohesion.

LIPIDS

Hair lipids divide into exogenous (sebum-derived: triglycerides, waxes, squalene, free fatty acids coating the cuticle) and endogenous (structural lipids integrated into the cell membrane and CMC - Cellular Membrane Complex, such as cholesterol and cholesterol sulfate).

Of fundamental structural relevance are phospholipids and ceramides, intrinsic constituents of cell membranes. Thanks to their amphipathic nature, these molecules modulate hydrophobicity and permeability, regulating water fluxes and ensuring correct hydration, elasticity, and intercellular cohesion between cuticular and cortical cells.

MELANINS

Melanin is the natural pigmented polymer localized predominantly in the cortex. It is synthesized within melanosomes by melanocytes located in the hair bulb, above the dermal papilla. Concentration, ratio, and density of these polymers determine the natural color spectrum (graying is due to cessation of melanogenesis and consequent pigment absence).

Two main macromolecular classes are distinguished:

  • EUMELANIN AND PHEOMELANIN: Eumelanin is an insoluble high molecular weight polymer, prevalent in dark phenotypes (brown and black). Pheomelanin is a sulfur-containing polymer, characteristic of light and warm tones (blonde and red).
  • TRICOCROMES: Specific polymeric variants of red hair (low molecular weight pigments containing iron, related to tricosiderin). The rutilian phenotype is regulated by recessive variants of the MC1R gene (melanocortin-1 receptor).

BIOSYNTHESIS OF MELANINS (MELANOGENESIS)

The common biochemical precursor is the amino acid L-tyrosine. In eumelanin synthesis, tyrosine is hydroxylated to L-DOPA and subsequently oxidized to dopachinone by the action of the tyrosinase enzyme (a copper-dependent enzyme). Through successive stages of cyclization and oxidative polymerization, dopachinone evolves into complex insoluble polymers. Catalytic efficiency determines pigment intensity (from light brown to absolute black).

The synthesis of pheomelanin occurs in the presence of a critical concentration of cysteine (or glutathione). Cysteine reacts rapidly with dopachinone to form cysteinyl-DOPA conjugates. These compounds undergo subsequent oxidations and polymerizations, generating a polymeric pigment whose color ranges from yellow to orange-red based on the stoichiometric ratio of precursors.

Oculocutaneous albinism represents a clinical model of dysfunction in this pathway: a genetic anomaly caused by mutations in the gene encoding the tyrosinase enzyme. Point or missense mutations alter the primary structure of the enzymatic protein (e.g., substituting key structural amino acids), causing total or partial loss of catalytic activity. The upstream metabolic block prevents tyrosine conversion, resulting in a total absence of melanin synthesis.

Natural fiber color is always the result of the co-presence and quantitative ratio between EUMELANIN, PHEOMELANIN, AND TRICOCROMES:

  • Eumelanin: Organized in large, ellipsoidal and granular melanosomes, distributed predominantly in the peripheral cortex. Selectively absorbs light, reflecting darker wavelengths; its dominance determines dark depth levels (blacks and browns).
  • Trichochromes: Intermediate chemical structure and medium granulometry, stably located in the cortical matrix. Confer characteristic warm, coppery, and saturated reflections typical of red hair.
  • Pheomelanin: Presented as small, spherical, and partially diffused melanosomes (diffuse pigment), arranged homogeneously even in the deepest areas of the cortex. Reflects yellow and orange wavelengths, determining the background of blonde and light hair.

The reciprocal percentage variation and volumetric density of these polymeric fractions within the cortex genetically define the entire scale of tones and shades of natural hair.
en.life_cycle_intro_anagen:
The hair follicle is a dynamic organ characterized by asynchronous cyclic activity, alternating between phases of growth, regression, and rest. Under physiological conditions, the shedding of a hair in the telogen phase coincides with the activation of a new anagen cycle within the same follicle. Phenomena of polytrichia (multiple shafts emerging from a single follicular ostium) predominantly occur within the follicular units of the scalp, where distinct follicles share a common sebaceous gland and hair canal.

The biological cycle of the hair is classically divided into three main phases: anagen, catagen, and telogen.

- Growth Phase (ANAGEN):

Anagen is divided into 6 subphases (from anagen I to anagen VI). The biological process begins with the activation of stem cells within the bulge niche and the matrix, stimulating intense mitotic activity until the complete formation of the new hair shaft. On the scalp, the anagen phase lasts an average of 2–4 years in men and 3–7 years in women. Consequently, the loss of 'mature' hair is compensated by the genesis of a new terminal strand. In other body regions, anagen is drastically shorter: 1–6 months for eyelashes and 20–40 days for body hair on limbs.

The duration of anagen is genetically determined and regulates the maximum length the hair can reach. The kinetic growth rate is approximately 1–1.5 cm per month, with generally higher rates in females. This phase varies by ethnicity (reaching its maximum in lissotrichous hair of Asian origin) and scalp topography: follicles in the occipital region (nape) exhibit a longer anagen phase compared to temporal ones. Concurrently, the vertical proliferation rate is statistically higher at the vertex than at the nape.
en.figure3_caption: Schematic representation of the hair follicle life cycle phases.
en.anagen_notes:
In follicles affected by androgenetic alopecia (baldness), a progressive temporal reduction of anagen is observed (down to just 3–5 months), associated with the phenomenon of follicular miniaturization, which limits the shaft length to a few centimeters and changes its structure to vellus hair. Biological aging (chronological aging) also physiologically reduces the duration of anagen and the shaft diameter.

Scientific Note: The replicative potential of the follicle is not mathematically predetermined at the local level, but is constrained by the reserve of stem cells and the longevity of the organism. Over the average human lifespan, a single follicle completes approximately 25–30 cycles before undergoing senescence.
en.cycle_catagen_telogen:
- Involution and Apoptosis Phase (CATAGEN):

It is a transient involutional phase lasting 2–4 weeks. During catagen, matrix keratinocytes undergo programmed apoptosis, melanin synthesis ceases, and the lower portion of the follicle resorbs, causing the dermal papilla to move upward toward the upper dermis (bulge zone).

- Functional Rest and Expulsion Phase (TELOGEN and EXOGEN):

Telogen represents the period of metabolic quiescence of the follicle, lasting an average of about 3 months. Mitotic activity is absent, and the anchoring sheaths keratinize, giving the base of the hair the typical 'club' structure. The actual detachment of the shaft from the follicle (now classified independently as the exogen phase) occurs physiologically when the new anagen strand mechanically pushes it out, ensuring the continuity of the hair turn-over.

If the follicle remains empty for a latent period between the expulsion of the old hair and the start of the new anagen, this is referred to as the kenogen phase. This empty interval, although present in minimal percentage in a healthy scalp, undergoes a significant and pathological increase in androgenetic alopecia.

Traumatic or mechanical extraction of a hair in telogen induces a biochemical signal that interrupts follicular quiescence, stimulating the early start of a new anagen. The characteristic whitish swelling visible at the base of spontaneously shed hairs is not the 'vital root', but the keratinized, pigment-free bulb typical of the telogen phase.

Unlike many mammals subject to synchronous molting, hair replacement in humans occurs in an asynchronous 'mosaic' pattern: each follicle evolves independently of adjacent ones. Systemic fluctuations and variations in photoperiod can, however, induce a partial synchronization of follicles, clinically evident as physiological seasonal effluvium during transition seasons (autumn and spring).
en.cycle_functional_summary:
From a strictly cyto-functional perspective, follicular activity is divided into two macro-phases:

  • - A proliferative and morphogenetic phase: characterized by the rapid downward extension of the keratinocytic column invading the deep dermis, corresponding to the initial subphases of anagen (from anagen I to anagen V);
  • - A differentiative and synthetic phase: predominant during anagen VI, where mitotic activity stabilizes at the basal level necessary to sustain continuous keratinization and protein synthesis of the shaft. The subsequent catagen represents mitotic arrest with terminal differentiation of residual cells, while telogen constitutes the biosynthetic silence necessary for structural reorganization of the follicular unit.

The temporal heterogeneity of the hair cycle in different anatomical areas (e.g., inverted anagen/telogen ratio in the pubic and eyebrow regions compared to the scalp) justifies the different biological lengths of body hairs and explains the selective susceptibility of follicles to specific dermatoses and immune or hormone-dependent pathologies.
en.atom_intro_components:
THE ATOM ⚛️

The atom is the smallest particle of a chemical element that retains its specific characteristics and identity.

Main Components

It consists of:
  • Nucleus: Composed of protons (positive charge) and neutrons (no charge). It concentrates almost all the mass of the atom (>99.9%).
  • Electrons: Negatively charged particles that move around the nucleus occupying orbitals, regions of space where the probability of finding them is highest (quantum mechanical model).
en.atom_ions_periodic:
Fundamental Concept

An electrically neutral atom has a number of protons equal to the number of electrons, resulting in a net charge = 0.

Ions 🧪

An ION is an atom (or a group of atoms) that has lost or gained one or more electrons, acquiring a net electric charge.

ANION
It forms by gaining electrons; the resulting charge is negative.
CATION
It forms by losing electrons; the resulting charge is positive.

Periodic Table of Elements 📊

Elements are arranged by increasing atomic number (Z), according to the periodicity principle formulated by Mendeleev in 1869.

Content of the Cell

  1. In the center: the chemical symbol (e.g., Na, Cl).
  2. Prominently displayed: the atomic number Z (number of protons; defines the element's identity).
  3. Usually at the bottom: the relative atomic mass (expressed in u, unified atomic mass unit; it is the weighted average of the masses of its natural isotopes).
en.bonds_stability_lewis_octet:
Chemical Bonds and Stability 🔗

Atoms interact to reach a state of minimum potential energy, forming more stable aggregates.

The bond forms spontaneously because the resulting system has a lower energy content than isolated atoms. Lower potential energy = greater stability.

Lewis Theory and Valence

Atoms interact to achieve a stable external electron configuration (minimum energy), often similar to that of noble gases.

The Octet Rule

Noble gases (Group 18) are chemically inert because they possess a complete valence shell: 8 electrons (octet), except for helium which is stable with 2 (duet).

Rule: “Atoms tend to lose, gain, or share electrons to reach the electron configuration of the nearest noble gas (8 electrons in the outer level, or 2 for H and He).”

Scientific Note: This rule is an excellent approximation for elements in periods 2 and 3, but presents exceptions (e.g., electron-deficient molecules, expanded octets in higher periods).
en.bond_energy_definition:
Bond Energy ⚡

Bond energy is the minimum energy required to break one mole of chemical bonds in the gaseous state (expressed in kJ/mol). It is a direct measure of bond strength: high values indicate strong bonds and thermodynamically stable molecules. The bond length is the equilibrium distance between the nuclei of bonded atoms.
en.bonds_ionic:
STRONG BONDS (INTRAMOLECULAR) 🔩

1. Ionic Bond

Definition: The electrostatic force of attraction between ions of opposite charge. It typically forms between atoms with a large electronegativity difference (△χ ≥ 1.7), resulting in an almost complete transfer of electron density.

HOW IT FORMS:

  1. There is a transfer of one or more valence electrons from one atom to another.
  2. The less electronegative atom (typically a metal) loses electrons, becoming a cation (positive ion).
  3. The more electronegative atom (typically a non-metal) gains electrons, becoming an anion (negative ion).

It does not form isolated molecules, but ordered three-dimensional geometric structures called crystalline lattices (e.g., Sodium Chloride, NaCl).
en.caption_ionico: CRYSTALLINE LATTICE FORMED BY Na<sup>+</sup> AND Cl<sup>-</sup> IONS HELD TOGETHER BY IONIC BONDS
en.bonds_covalent:
2. Covalent Bond

It occurs through the sharing of electrons between non-metal atoms with similar or identical electronegativity (△χ < 1.7).

HOW IT FORMS:

The two atoms share one or more pairs (doublets) of electrons in molecular orbitals, allowing each atom to complete its valence shell (octet rule).*

Types of Covalent Bonds:

Pure (or Nonpolar):
It forms between identical atoms or those with negligible electronegativity difference (△χ ≤ 0.4). The electron cloud is distributed symmetrically between the two nuclei (e.g., H2, O2, CH4).
Polar:
It forms between atoms with different electronegativity (0.4 < △χ < 1.7). Bonding electrons are attracted more strongly to the more electronegative atom, generating a partial negative charge (δ-) on it and a partial positive charge (δ+) on the other. The molecule thus constitutes an electric dipole (e.g., HCl, H2O).
Coordinate (or Dative):
The shared electron pair originates entirely from one atom (donor, possessing a lone pair) and is accepted into an empty orbital of the other atom (acceptor). Once formed, it is experimentally indistinguishable from a common covalent bond; it is often represented graphically with an arrow (→) (e.g., H3O+, NH4+).

*Note: Exceptions to the octet rule exist, such as the incomplete octet (e.g., Boron compounds), the expanded octet (e.g., SF6), or molecules with an odd number of electrons.
en.caption_covalent: Covalent bond: sharing of valence electron pairs between atoms to achieve electronic stability.
en.bonds_metallic:
3. Metallic Bond ⚙️

It is described by the "electron sea" model (or Drude-Lorentz model): the structure consists of a geometric lattice of positive ions (fixed) immersed in a diffuse cloud of highly delocalized valence electrons that are free to move throughout the solid.

The cohesion of the metallic bond is guaranteed by the omnidirectional electrostatic attraction between the shared ("mobile") valence electrons and the positive ionic cores.
en.caption_metallic: Metallic bond model: metal cations stabilized by a sea of delocalized electrons, responsible for conductivity and malleability properties.
en.bonds_weak:
WEAK BONDS (Intermolecular Forces) 💧

Weak bonds have a bond energy significantly lower than interatomic (intramolecular) bonds, but are fundamental in determining the macroscopic properties and physical state of molecular compounds.

Types:

  • van der Waals forces: include dipole-dipole interactions (between polar molecules) and London dispersion forces (present in all molecules, but dominant in non-polar ones).
  • Hydrogen Bond (a particularly strong type of dipole-dipole interaction).

Hydrogen Bond

It occurs when a hydrogen atom, covalently bonded to a highly electronegative and small-sized element (such as fluorine, oxygen, or nitrogen), is attracted to the lone electron pair of another electronegative atom in a neighboring molecule.

It manifests in highly polar molecules like water (H2O) and ammonia (NH3). While the strongest hydrogen bonds can reach up to 50 kJ/mol (e.g., in HF), the hydrogen bonds in water typically range between 20–25 kJ/mol. This is still significantly weaker than an intramolecular covalent bond (e.g., the O-H bond requires approx. 460 kJ/mol to break).

In ice, hydrogen bonds force molecules to arrange in a tetrahedral geometry that generates a very open hexagonal crystalline lattice. This increases the volume and reduces the density of the solid compared to the liquid, allowing ice to float. In the liquid phase, these bonds constantly break and reform, giving water high cohesion, high surface tension, high specific heat, and elevated phase transition points.
en.reactions:
CHEMICAL REACTIONS: Types and Representation 🧪

A chemical reaction is a process involving the breaking and/or formation of chemical bonds, resulting in the redistribution of atoms and the transformation of reactants into products with different chemical properties.

Chemical Equation

A chemical equation is the qualitative and quantitative representation of a reaction, where reactants are written on the left and products on the right:

REACTANTS → PRODUCTS

Classification of Reactions (by Mechanism)

  1. Synthesis: Two or more substances (elements or compounds) combine to form a single, more complex compound.
    General Equation: A + B → AB
  2. Decomposition: A compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances, typically requiring an input of energy (thermal, electrical, or light energy, known respectively as thermolysis, electrolysis, or photolysis).
    General Equation: AB → A + B
  3. Single Displacement (or Substitution): A free element, being more reactive, replaces and displaces another less reactive element present in a compound.
    General Equation: A + BC → AC + B
  4. Double Displacement: Two compounds in solution exchange their respective constituents (ions) to form two new compounds (often accompanied by the formation of a precipitate, a gas, or water).
    General Equation: AB + CD → AD + CB

Thermodynamics and Reversibility

  • Reversible Reactions: Reactions in which products can react with each other to reform the reactants, reaching a state of dynamic equilibrium (where the rate of the forward reaction is equal to that of the reverse reaction). They are represented by a double reaction arrow: ⇌
  • Irreversible Reactions: Reactions that proceed almost entirely towards product formation, until the limiting reactant is exhausted. Stable products do not tend to spontaneously recombine in a significant manner to restore the reactants.
en.inorganic:
INORGANIC COMPOUNDS 🔬

They are chemical substances typically characterized by the absence of carbon-hydrogen (C-H) bonds. Unlike organic compounds, most do not contain carbon; however, certain carbon-containing species (such as carbon oxides, carbonates, bicarbonates, carbides, and cyanides) are classified as inorganic due to their chemical properties and behavior, which are similar to mineral salts.

Chemical Functions and Nomenclature

1. Basic Oxides

Binary compounds formed by a metal and oxygen.

Naming: [Metal Name] + Oxide. If the metal has multiple oxidation states, it is specified using Roman numerals (e.g., Iron(II) oxide) or classical suffixes (e.g., Ferrous oxide).

Examples:
ZnO → Zinc oxide
MgO → Magnesium oxide

2. Acidic Oxides (Acid Anhydrides)

Binary compounds formed by a non-metal and oxygen.

Naming: In modern IUPAC nomenclature: prefix + oxide of + [non-metal]. In classical nomenclature: [Non-metal] + anhydride.

Examples:
CO2 → Carbon dioxide (Carbonic anhydride)
SO3 → Sulfur trioxide (Sulfuric anhydride)

3. Hydroxides (Bases)

They derive from the hydration reaction of a basic oxide:
Basic Oxide + H2O → Hydroxide

Their structure is characterized by the presence of one or more hydroxyl groups (OH-) bonded to a metal cation.

Examples:
MgO + H2O → Mg(OH)2 (Magnesium hydroxide)
CaO + H2O → Ca(OH)2 (Calcium hydroxide)

Brønsted-Lowry Definition: A base is a chemical species capable of accepting a proton (H+) from another species.

ACIDS

Inorganic acids are classified based on the presence of oxygen:

  1. Oxoacids (Acidic oxides + H2O): They contain oxygen in their structure. They are formed by the reaction: Anhydride + H2O → Oxoacid.
    Example: CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 (Carbonic acid).
  2. Binary Acids (Non-metal + H): They do not contain oxygen. The general formula is HnX, where X is a non-metal.
    Example: HCl (Hydrochloric acid in aqueous solution; hydrogen chloride in gaseous state).

Brønsted-Lowry Definition: An acid is a chemical species capable of donating a proton (H+) to another species.

SALTS

Salts are neutral ionic compounds resulting from the neutralization reaction between an acid and a base.

Acid + Hydroxide → Salt + Water

Example:
HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H2O
(Hydrochloric acid + Sodium hydroxide → Sodium chloride + Water)
en.mixtures:
MIXTURES AND DISPERSED SYSTEMS ⚗️

A mixture is a physicochemical system consisting of the coexistence of two or more pure substances that do not react with each other. Dispersed systems, defined by the presence of a dispersing (continuous) phase and a dispersed phase, are classified based on their degree of dispersion and thermodynamic homogeneity:

  1. Heterogeneous Systems (Heterogeneous Mixtures and Colloids): Polyphasic systems in which the individual constituents retain their physical interfaces and macroscopic properties. They include suspensions, emulsions, foams, aerosols (mists and smokes), and colloidal systems.
  2. Homogeneous Systems (True Solutions): Monophasic systems at the molecular or ionic level, in which the individual components are neither optically nor physically distinguishable.

Systems and Macro- and Microscopic Heterogeneity

1. SUSPENSIONS:
Biphasic systems that are only kinetically stable, consisting of a solid dispersed phase (particles with diameter > 1 µm) insoluble in a liquid dispersing phase. The particles are subject to gravitational sedimentation, described by Stokes' law, which can be countered by modifying the viscosity of the continuous medium.
2. EMULSIONS:
Thermodynamically unstable systems composed of two immiscible or partially miscible liquid phases. They require the action of emulsifying agents (surfactants) that reduce interfacial tension and prevent instability phenomena (coalescence, flocculation, creaming).
  • O/W (Oil-in-Water): Lipophilic phase (discontinuous) dispersed in a hydrophilic phase (continuous). Characteristic of stable hydrating emulsions, hydrophilic cosmetics, and rapid-absorption systems.
  • W/O (Water-in-Oil): Hydrophilic phase (discontinuous) dispersed in a lipophilic phase (continuous). Typical systems for barrier creams, ointments, and high-persistence cutaneous formulations.
3. FOAMS:
Fluid dispersed systems in which the dispersing phase is liquid (or solid) and the dispersed phase is gaseous, stabilized by interfacial surfactant films.
4. AEROSOLS (MISTS AND SMOKES):
Systems in which the dispersing phase is a gas. Mist is a liquid aerosol (dispersed droplets), while smoke is a solid aerosol (dispersed solid particulate matter).

Formulative Example (O/W Hydrating Cream):

The structured emulsifying network (Polyglyceryl-4-Stearate, Cetearyl Glucoside) stabilizes the thermodynamically unstable interface of the O/W system. Amphiphilic consistency agents, such as Cetyl Alcohol, co-emulsify and modify the rheology of the system, while Cera Alba acts as a lipidic consistency factor, modifying both the rheological behavior and the final texture.

Monophasic Systems and Colloidal Systems

1. TRUE SOLUTIONS:
Thermodynamically stable homogeneous (monophasic) systems, in which the solute is dispersed in the solvent at a molecular or ionic level (dimensions < 1 nm).

The solubility represents the maximum concentration of solute that can dissolve in a solvent at a defined temperature and pressure, reaching thermodynamic equilibrium. A solution is defined as saturated when a dynamic equilibrium is established between the dissolved solute and the undissolved excess (solid body).

Note: The dissolution process is thermodynamically temperature-dependent: generally endothermic for solids (solubility increases with T) and exothermic for gases.

2. COLLOIDAL SYSTEMS (COLLOIDAL DISPERSIONS):
Micro-heterogeneous systems characterized by particles with linear dimensions between 1 and 1000 nm. While displaying prolonged kinetic stability and macroscopic uniformity, they possess an extremely high interfacial free energy.
They manifest unique optical properties, including the Tyndall effect (light scattering or Rayleigh/Mie scattering by particles dispersed in the dispersing phase).
Classification: Lyophilic (high affinity and solvation of the dispersed phase, thermodynamically stable), lyophobic (poor affinity, unstable unless stabilized by electrostatic charges or steric barriers), and association colloids.

Association Colloids and Phase Transitions

These are systems generated by the spontaneous self-aggregation of amphiphilic molecules (surfactants) possessing a hydrophilic portion (polar head) and a hydrophobic portion (non-polar tail). Upon reaching the Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC), these molecules organize into aggregated structures called micelles, reducing the free energy of the system.
  • GEL: A high-viscosity biphasic colloidal system in which the solid phase forms a continuous three-dimensional network that traps and immobilizes the liquid phase (viscoelastic behavior).
  • SOL: A fluid colloidal suspension in which solid particles are freely dispersed in the continuous liquid phase.
en.ph_academic:
pH: Definition, Thermodynamics, and Biological Importance 🧬

The pH (potential of hydrogen) is a logarithmic scale quantifying the acidity or alkalinity of aqueous solutions. It is thermodynamically defined as the negative common logarithm of the activity of hydronium ions (H3O+):

pH = -log10(aH3O+) = -log10(γ · [H3O+])

Where γ is the activity coefficient. In very dilute solutions (γ → 1), activity approximates molar concentration: pH ≈ -log10[H3O+]. It reflects the dynamic equilibrium between H3O+ and OH−, regulated by the ionic product of water (Kw).

Scale and Classification

  • Scale: Conventionally between 0 (maximum acidity) and 14 (maximum alkalinity) in dilute aqueous solutions at 25°C, although in highly concentrated systems thermodynamic values may exceed these limits.
  • Neutral: pH = 7 at 25°C (e.g., pure water, where the activity of H3O+ equals that of OH−).
  • Acidic: pH < 7 ([H3O+] > [OH−]). A Brønsted-Lowry acid is a chemical species capable of donating protons (H+) to the solvent.
  • Alkaline (or Basic): pH > 7 ([H3O+] < [OH−]). A Brønsted-Lowry base is a species capable of accepting protons from the solvent.

Biological Relevance and Homeostasis

Even minor pH variations are critical for the human body, composed of approximately 60–70% water. Metabolic reactions and enzymatic activity depend strictly on maintaining ion concentrations within extremely narrow physiological ranges (homeostasis).

A physiological blood pH of approximately 7.4 (range: 7.35–7.45) is essential for the structure, electric charge, and function of proteins and cell membranes. Alterations in intracellular pH and organelle pH (e.g., endosomes and lysosomes) are correlated with pathologies such as neoplasms, neurodegenerative diseases, and metabolic disorders. The endo-lysosomal system, in particular, requires progressive acidification (to pH values of approximately 4.5–5.0) for the correct disposal and enzymatic degradation of cellular waste.
en.ph_cutaneo:
Skin pH, Eudermia, and Defense 🛡️

Cosmetics, applied to the skin surface (in compliance with EU Cosmetic Regulations), interact directly with the stratum corneum and the hydrolipidic film. It is therefore essential that their pH is compatible with the physiological skin pH to ensure eudermia (non-irritancy and respect for the barrier function).

The Acid Mantle and Skin Barrier

The epidermis is protected by a superficial acid mantle, a primary defense mechanism that regulates microbial colonization and prevents transepidermal water loss (TEWL).

  • Skin (Body): pH between 4.5 and 6.0 (optimal average: 5.5).
  • Scalp: pH between 4.2 and 5.0.

Consequences of Inappropriate pH

Neutral-Alkaline Environment (pH ≥ 6.0):
Shifting towards neutral values alters the microbiota equilibrium. Cutibacterium acnes, while tolerating physiological pH, increases lipase production and virulence (inflammatory phenotypes) and promotes the proliferation of pathogens like Staphylococcus aureus when pH exceeds 6.0. The integrity of the skin barrier is compromised, altering the hydrolipidic film, inducing dehydration, dryness, hypersensitivity, and accelerating skin aging processes.
Excessively Acidic Environment (pH < 4.5):
Can alter the commensal bacterial flora (skin microbiota), weakening the natural biological defense. It may cause irritation, erythema, itching, and impairment of the barrier function.
en.ph_formulation:
Ideal pH by Product Category 🧪

Determining the ideal pH is crucial for skin integrity and formula stability. Below are the optimal (eudermic) pH values for major cosmetic categories:

Target Value Table (pH Range)

ProductTarget pH
Face / Body Cream5.5 – 6.0
Face Cleansing Milk6.0 – 7.0
Face Toner4.5 – 5.5
Face / Body Exfoliant (High Concentration)~3.5
Face / Body Exfoliant (Sensitive Skin)~4.5
Eye Contour6.5 – 7.2 (Compatible with tear film ~7.4)
Body Wash5.5 – 6.5
Intimate Hygiene Product4.0 – 4.5 (Higher protective acidity)
Hair Shampoo4.5 – 5.0
Hair Conditioner~4.5
Deodorant (Underarm)4.0 – 5.0
Traditional Soap9.5 – 10.5 (Marked alkalinity)

pH Adjustment in Formulation 🧪

pH correction requires specific agents depending on the desired direction. To raise the pH (alkalinization), strong bases like Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH) or Potassium Hydroxide (KOH) are used, or buffers like Sodium Bicarbonate (effective up to pH ~8.3). To lower the pH (acidification), weak organic acids such as Citric Acid, Lactic Acid, or Glycolic Acid are employed. The use of vinegar (Acetic Acid) is discouraged in stable formulations due to organic impurities and persistent odor.

Corrections require precision: add the regulating agent drop by drop under stirring, measuring the pH after each aliquot to avoid overshoot (excessive imbalance). The safest and most effective standardized stock solutions are expressed in % w/w (weight/weight), totaling 100 g:

  • Citric Acid 20% (w/w): 20 g of citric acid dissolved in purified water up to a total weight of 100 g (i.e., 80 g of water).
  • Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH) 10-18% (w/w): 10-18 g of NaOH dissolved in purified water up to a total weight of 100 g. Note: Handle with PPE (gloves, goggles) as it is highly corrosive.
  • Sodium Bicarbonate 5-10% (w/w): 5-10 g of bicarbonate dissolved in purified water up to a total weight of 100 g. Useful as a mild buffer.
en.desc_ph_capelli:
pH and Hair: Electrostatics and Cuticle Integrity 💇

Hair shaft health is governed by pH, which determines the net electric charge of the surface and the opening state of the cuticular scales. Hair possesses a deep chemical isoelectric point (pI) between 3.67 and 4.5, while the surface iso-charge point is around 5.0, intervals in which the positive and negative charges of keratin reach equilibrium.

Physiological State and Cuticular Closure

  • Physiological pH (4.5 – 5.5): In this weakly acidic interval, the net surface electrical charge is minimal. Therefore, inter-slit electrostatic repulsions are reduced to basal levels.
  • Structural Integrity: The cuticle scales adhere tightly to the shaft ("closed" configuration). This reduces dynamic friction, maximizes specular light reflection (shine), and limits the loss of water and endogenous proteins (cortex).

Mechanism of Alkalinity Damage

At a pH above 7 (alkaline), the carboxylic (–COOH) and thiol groups of keratin undergo a marked deprotonation, generating a strong negative net electric charge. An electrostatic repulsion phenomenon is established between the negatively charged scales, causing:

  • Cuticle Lifting: The scales geometrically dissociate by opening up, and the fiber undergoes structural swelling due to the massive entry of water.
  • Mechanical Degradation: An increase in inter-fiber friction (frizz), high porosity, loss of elastic modulus, and a severe susceptibility to breakage and physical stripping of the scales during brushing processes are recorded.

INCI Regulations and Labeling

The labeling of formulations must comply with Regulation (EC) No 1223/2009, using the official International Nomenclature Cosmetic Ingredient (INCI) nomenclature. The order of declaration follows strict macroscopic rules:

  1. Decreasing Order of Weight: Ingredients are listed in decreasing order of weight at the time of their addition into the manufacturing batch (from highest to lowest concentration).
  2. 1% Cut-Off Threshold: Ingredients present in concentrations below 1% w/w may be listed in an arbitrary order, provided they are positioned after those with a concentration above 1%.
  3. Cosmetic Colorants: They can be listed in an arbitrary order at the end of the ingredient list regardless of their concentration, identified by the Colour Index code (e.g., CI 42090).

Functional Classes of Ingredients

Raw materials are academically classified according to their rheological or technological function in the formulation system:

  • Surfactants: Surface-active agents (anionic, amphoteric, non-ionic, cationic) with cleansing, foaming, or wetting functions.
  • Emulsifiers: Lipophilic or hydrophilic surfactants designed to stabilize thermodynamically unstable biphasic systems (O/W or W/O).
  • Rheology Modifiers: Synthetic, semi-synthetic polymers or hydrocolloid gums that modulate viscosity and yield stress.
  • Humectants: Poly-titrated hygroscopic compounds (e.g., Glycerin, Propylene Glycol) that retain free water for topical hydration and packaging stability.
  • Lipids and Emollients: Vegetable triglyceride oils, anhydrous butters, synthetic esters, and fluid silicones for lipid film restoration.
  • Preservative Systems: Auxiliary substances with antimicrobial action aimed at ensuring microbiological stability over time.
  • Antioxidants: Kinetic inhibitors of free radicals to prevent rancidity of the lipid phase (e.g., Tocopherol).
  • Sequestering Agents (Chelators): Coordinating compounds (e.g., EDTA, Phytic Acid) that complex polyvalent metal cations, preventing oxidative instability or precipitation phenomena.
  • Functional Substances (Actives): Ingredients endowed with a specific biological, clinical, or cosmetic activity targeted at the biological target.
en.capelli_introduzione_tecnica:
Hair Formulations: Chemistry and Functionality 💇‍♀️

Origin of Raw Materials and Formulation Philosophies

Cosmetic ingredients can be of mineral, animal, vegetable, or synthetic origin (produced in a laboratory), which currently constitute most components to ensure stability and reproducibility.

US Philosophy:
Historically oriented toward stability, sensory pleasure, and performance, with regulations that favor the use of high-performance synthetic or mineral ingredients.
European Philosophy (EU Reg. 1223/2009):
Adopts the precautionary principle, with strict regulations on toxicological safety, sustainability, and biodegradability. It drives toward the use of "green" or natural ingredients, while maintaining the use of synthetic ones where they ensure superior safety and stability.

Role of Denatured Alcohol

Denatured alcohol (ethanol with bittering agents such as denatonium) is a ubiquitous component with multiple technological functions:

  • Solvent: Dissolves resins, fats, and organic substances insoluble in water.
  • Excipient and Carrier: Facilitates the penetration of active ingredients into the shaft and provides a rapid cooling sensation due to evaporation.
  • Antiseptic and Co-solvent: Reduces microbial load and lowers viscosity in gel formulations, improving fluidity.

Sprays, Hairsprays, and Propellants

Modern propellants used to atomize products are light hydrocarbons (Butane, Isobutane, Propane), which replaced CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) banned by the Montreal Protocol due to their impact on the ozone layer. Modern propellants have an ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential) = 0, although they are classified as VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds) with a potential global warming impact.

In hairsprays, alongside alcohols, volatile solvents (e.g., ethers or acetates) are used to facilitate rapid evaporation and film formation. Chlorinated solvents (e.g., dichloromethane) are now almost absent due to their high toxicity and regulatory restrictions.

Functional Additives, Preservatives, and Reinforcers

  • Vitamins: Niacinamide (Vitamin B₃) is a precursor to NAD/NADP coenzymes, essential for cellular energy metabolism and ceramide synthesis, strengthening the barrier and shine.
  • Panthenol: Provitamin B₅. When applied to the scalp, it converts enzymatically into pantothenic acid (a component of Coenzyme A); on the hair shaft, it acts instead as a powerful hygroscopic humectant, improving hydration, elasticity, and mechanical strength.
  • Preservatives and Boosters: Systems such as still-allowed long-chain parabens (e.g., Butylparaben, strictly restricted to a maximum of 0.14% cumulative according to EU Reg.) and Propylene Glycol (multifunctional glycol: humectant and "booster" of the preservative system that reduces water activity, aw, without being an active preservative listed in Annex V).

Gels, Foams, and Fixing Power

Gels are water/alcohol-based fixatives with high polymer content, while Mousses (foams) offer a lighter hold thanks to their aerated structure.

  • Fatty Excipients: Often vegetable derivatives (e.g., castor oil) to give plasticity to the film and reduce the "flaking" effect.
  • Thickeners (Gums): Cellulose derivatives (Hydroxyethylcellulose, Hydroxypropylcellulose) increase the viscosity of the aqueous phase, stabilize the foam, and enhance the fixing action.
en.capelli_didascalia_img: Layered arrangement of PVP (inner) and polydimethylsiloxane (outer) on the hair.
en.capelli_agenti_e_tinture:
Film-Forming Agents and the Natural Effect

Film-formers are polymers that form a continuous film on the shaft, providing body, texture, and smoothness. The technology has evolved from rigid polymers to hybrid systems.

Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP): Historic hydrophilic polymer. It creates a rigid, crystalline film that is water-soluble. It is responsible for the classic wet look and the crunchy feeling in high humidity.
